coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from hawthorn for the rapid determination of chlortetracycline in pork samples
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Huifang
Zhou, Qian
Han, Xue
Li, Ming
Yuan, Jing
Wei, Ran
Zhang, Xiaofu
Wu, Mengying
Zhao, Wen
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, the methods for rapidly detecting antibiotics using the unique fluorescent properties of carbon dots have attracted increasing attentions. The purpose of this study is to prepare carbon dots by hawthorn and establish a rapid fluorescence sensor for the detection of chlortetracycline in pork samples. An environmentally friendly nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with hawthorn powder as the carbon source and diethylenetriamine (DETA) as the nitrogen source was synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal process. The preparation conditions of the N-CDs were optimized. Subsequently, the characteristics of the N-CDs were elucidated by fluorescence, UV absorption, transmission electron microscope, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The N-CDs showed a quantum yield of 22.96%, emission at 447 nm at the maximum excitation wavelength of 370 nm and an average particle diameter of 3.17 nm. Meanwhile, the factors affecting the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs were studied. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching method for detecting chlortetracycline in pork was established and optimized. Under the best experimental conditions, the linear range (R2 = 0.9992) was developed over 0.4–20 μg mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.073 ± 0.005 μg mL−1 (S/N = 3). Chlortetracycline in pork samples had been successfully detected with good recoveries of 93.62%−103.18%, which suggested that the study provided a new approach for the detection of chlortetracycline in pork.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots
Hawthorn
Chlortetracycline detection
Fluorescence sensor

Building up the assay for CTC
Linear range and detection limit of CTC
Pork sample testing and recovery rate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis and application of N-CDs.

Fluorescence excitation and emission spectra and UV spectrum of N-CDs. Inset: photographs of N-CDs solution (A); emission spectra of N-CDs for varying excitation wavelengths (B); TEM images of N-CDs (C); size distribution of N-CDs (D).
XPS spectrum, high resolution C1s, O1s, and N1 s XPS spectra of the N-CDs.
Optimizing the analysis conditions of CTC detection by N-CDs including pH (A), the amount of BR buffer (B), the concentration of N-CDs (C), temperature (D) and time (E).
The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreases with increasing of CTC concentration (0, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 20 μg mL−1) (A); linear relationship between F/F0 and various CTC concentrations (B).

F/F0 of the reaction system after the addition of different types of antibiotics (A) and various inorganic compounds (B). CTC: chlortetracycline, OTC: oxytetracycline, TC: tetracycline, DC: doxycycline, CAP: Chloramphenicol, SM: sulfanilamide, FF: florfenicol.
UV absorption spectrum of CTC with excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs (A); UV absorption spectra for CTC, N-CDs with CTC and N-CDs (B).
Comparison the analytical performance of different methods for CTC detection.

row.entry: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots from rhizobiumsilicon quantum dots
high performance liquid chromatography with ultraviolet and fluorescence detection
a Solid SERS Substrate Based on Self-assembled Gold Nanobipyramids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recoveries of CTC supplemented in pork samples detected by the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen-doped carbon dots derived from hawthorn for the rapid determination of chlortetracycline in pork samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Technology, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding 071000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Food Science and Technology, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding 071000, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Science, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding 071000, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Science, Hebei Agricultural University, Baoding 071000, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The purpose of this study is to prepare carbon dots by hawthorn and establish a rapid fluorescence sensor for the detection of chlortetracycline in pork samples. A simple, green method to synthesize nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) by hawthorn was developed. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching method for detecting chlortetracycline in pork was established. Chlortetracycline in pork samples had been successfully detected with good recoveries.

In recent years, the methods for rapidly detecting antibiotics using the unique fluorescent properties of carbon dots have attracted increasing attentions. The purpose of this study is to prepare carbon dots by hawthorn and establish a rapid fluorescence sensor for the detection of chlortetracycline in pork samples. An environmentally friendly nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with hawthorn powder as the carbon source and diethylenetriamine (DETA) as the nitrogen source was synthesized by one-pot hydrothermal process. The preparation conditions of the N-CDs were optimized. Subsequently, the characteristics of the N-CDs were elucidated by fluorescence, UV absorption, transmission electron microscope, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy. The N-CDs showed a quantum yield of 22.96%, emission at 447 nm at the maximum excitation wavelength of 370 nm and an average particle diameter of 3.17 nm. Meanwhile, the factors affecting the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs were studied. Moreover, the fluorescence quenching method for detecting chlortetracycline in pork was established and optimized. Under the best experimental conditions, the linear range (R2 = 0.9992) was developed over 0.4–20 μg mL−1 with a detection limit of 0.073 ± 0.005 μg mL−1 (S/N = 3). Chlortetracycline in pork samples had been successfully detected with good recoveries of 93.62%−103.18%, which suggested that the study provided a new approach for the detection of chlortetracycline in pork.

ce:section.ce:para: Chlortetracycline (CTC), a broad-spectrum tetracycline antibiotic, is widely used in animal husbandry as a veterinary medicine and a feed additive [1-3]. However, a large number of such antibiotic feed and drugs are employed toraise livestock by many farmers due to the good antibacterial effect and low price [4,5]. The abuse of CTC has resulted in serious CTC residues in animal food [6]. Many countries have strict regulations on CTC residues in livestock products. For example, the maximum residue limits (MRLs) of United States for CTC in muscle, liver and kidney of bovine, ovine and porcine tissues are 2, 6 and 12 mg kg−1, respectively. The European Union and China have the same MRLs for muscle, liver and kidney of all edible animals of 0.1, 0.3 and 0.6 mg kg−1, respectively [1,7,8]. In addition, China has comprehensively banned the addition of antibiotics in feed since 2020. In this case, the detection of chlortetracycline faces a more severe situation.

At present, the detection methods of CTC mainly include high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [9], liquid chromatograph with tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [10], microbial method [11] and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [12], etc. These methods have high sensitivity and accuracy. Nevertheless, HPLC and LC-MS methods have the downside of expensive and complicated equipment and time consuming sample pretreatment, and the ELISA and microbial methods have the possibility of cross-reactions and false positives [13]. Therefore, it is necessary to explore further more simple, time-saving and sensitive methods for detecting CTC content.

Carbon dots (CDs) are a new kind of fluorescent nanomaterial with fantastic sizes less than 10 nm [14-16]. It provides many great properties, such as adjustable photoluminescence, low photobleaching, low toxicity, low cost, biocompatibility and chemical stability [17,18]. Thereby, these advantages have resulted in CDs with plenty of applications in the fields of analytical detection, nanomedicine, biosensing, and imaging [19]. To improve their properties, other atoms different from carbon and oxygen are usually introduced into the structure of CDs. Nitrogen, sulphur and phosphorus have been the most commonly used elements [20].

Biomass carbon dots are a kind of CDs which have advantages of eco-friendly, low cost and inexhaustible supplying [21-23]. In recent years, CDs have been prepared by natural substances such as leaves, pork chop soup, strawberry, roses and so on [24]. During hydrothermal carbonisation, natural substances react in water under high sealing pressure. The components like carbohydrates can hydrolyze, forming oligomers and glucose [25]. These intermediary products can further undergo complex pathways to form a crystalline nanoparticle core [26]. In addition, the existence of oxygen-rich functional groups is expected to provide versatile anchoring points for doping agents to improve the performance of carbon dots [27]. Recently, carbon dots have been used more and more frequently in the detection of antibiotic residues in animal-derived food. Fluorescence quenching effect or fluorescence labeling is used to achieve rapid and accurate detection of the target. This provides an interesting idea for the goal of food safety testing from the laboratory to the table. It also provides more convenient and effective methods for food inspection in large quantities [27,28]. Therefore, it is necessary to establish the detection method of antibiotic residue in animal-derived food by carbon dots.

Hawthorn is a traditional fruit that is popular in Asian countries [29]. However, there are a lot of losses in picking, transportation and processing, so it is worth thinking about how to make full use of the obsolete hawthorn fruits. In this thesis, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) were produced from hawthorn with DETA as the doping agent via a one-step hydrothermal method for the detection of CTC. This paper introduced a fast, sensitive and straightforward fluorescent sensor to detect CTC in pork. Altogether, this work combined a fast detection with harmless raw materials has broadened a new approach for the preparation of fluorescent materials and antibiotics detecting.

The hawthorn fruits were bought from the local market (Baoding, China). Diethylenetriamine (DETA) was obtained from Shanghai Acmec Biochemical Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Chlortetracycline, tetracycline, oxytetracycline, doxycycline, chloramphenicol, sulfanilamide, florfenicol were purchased from Beijing Solarbio Science & Technology Co. Ltd. (Beijing, China). Britton Robinson (BR) buffer solutions ranged from pH 2.0 to 10.0. All solutions were prepared in ultrapure water. All chemicals were of analytical grade.

Fluorescence data were examined on a Gangdong F-320 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Tianjin, China). Furthermore, Ultraviolet–Visible absorption was recorded by a UNIC UV-2802H spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). N-CDs’appearance image was obtained by Tecnai G2 F20 Field-emission transmission electron microscope (FEI company, America). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was recorded by SHIMADZU IRprestige-21 spectrometer (Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by a Kratos Analytical AXIS Supra photoelectron spectrometer (UK). The powder of hawthorn and N-CDs were obtained by lyophilisation in PiloFD8-4.3 V (California, USA).

The N-CDs were prepared by one-step hydrothermal process of hawthorn fruit’s freeze-dried powder using stainless-steel autoclave as previously described with some modifications (Fig. 1 ) [26,30]. Firstly, 0.25 g of powdered hawthorn, 300 μL of DETA and 20 mL of ultrapure water were put into a teflon liner of the autoclave and treated in ultrasonic for 10 min. Then, the sample solution was heated at 180 °C for 5 h. After heating, the produced solution was exposured to room temperature and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min. The dispersion was filtered with 0.22 μm filter membranes. Then, the solution was washed with dichloromethane and purified by ultrapure water through a dialysis membrane (1000 MWCO) for 48 h. The produced solution was stored at 4 °C or lyophilized for characterization.

The experimental conditions had been respectively optimized, including the dosage of hawthorn powder (0.25, 0.5, 0.75 and 1.0 g), the dosage of DETA (100, 200, 300 and 400 μL), reaction time (2, 4, 6, 8, 10 and 12 h) and reaction temperature (120, 140, 160, 180 and 200 ℃). The other steps were the same as above.

Quinine sulfate (QY is 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4 solution) as standard was used to determine the QY of N-CDs, which was dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 [31]. The absorption values and fluorescent spectra were obtained. Then, the QY of N-CDs was calculated by the following equation:

Where N-CDs and st are the N-CDs and reference standard. Q is to the QY, I is the integrated photoluminescence (PL) intensity, A is the absorbance measured at the excited wavelength and η is the refractive index.

50 μL N-CDs, 1 mL of BR buffer solution and CTC with different concentrations were placed in a 5 mL volumetric flask, and then diluted to 5 mL with ultrapure water. After mixed together, the fluorescence intensity of the solution was obtained under the excitation of 370 nm.

Referring to the above steps, the conditions for detecting CTC were optimized, including pH value (pH 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10), the amount of BR buffer (0.5, 1, 1.5, 2 mL), the concentration of N-CDs (dilution factor 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180, 200), temperature (15, 25, 35, 45, 55 ℃) and time (0, 10, 20, 30, 40 min) of reactions.

Chloramphenicol, sulfanilamide, florfenicol, chlortetracycline, tetracycline, oxytetracycline and doxycycline were evaluated with the N-CDs by the same steps as CTC detection. Additionally, K+, Zn2+, Na+, Cu2+, Ca2 +, Al3+, Fe3+, Cl-, CO3 2–, SO42- were added to the reaction system to assess the selectivity of the developed method, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CTC content in pork samples
ce:section.ce:para: Fresh pork samples were collected from local supermarket for the spike recovery experiment. The fluorescence emission spectra were analyzed at an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The standard analyte (CTC) was added to the sample after weighing the sample and resting for 10 min. Extraction and purification of CTC in pork were performed using a modified version of Anderson Shanoy C. et al [2] and Xu N. et al [32]. 5 g of the pork mud sample (accurate to 0.01 g) was weighted in 50 mL centrifuge tube and 20 mL Na2EDTA-Mcllvainc buffer (100 mM). The sample was oscillated for 1 min, extracted for 10 min in an ultrasonic bath, and centrifuged 3000 rpm for 10 min (temperatures below 15 ℃) for three repeated times. Then the pock extract was filtered by a filter paper. Finally, the collected solutions were cleaned up by solid-phase extraction (Waters Oasis HLB cartridge, Massachusetts, USA).

In this study, hawthorn powder was used to prepare N-CDs by one-step hydrothermal method with DETA as the doping agent (Fig. 1). The formation and fluorescence characteristics of N-CDs could be affected by the dosage of hawthorn powder, the dosage of DETA, the temperature and time of reactions. Firstly, the effect of the dosage of hawthorn powder (0.25, 0.5, 0.75 and 1.0 g) on the fluorescence intensity was researched. As shown in Fig. S1A, as the dosages of hawthorn powder increased, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs had a downward trend. When the dosages of hawthorn powder were 0.75 and 1 g, the fluorescence intensities were similar and relatively low. At 0.25 and 0.5 g, the fluorescence intensities were also similar but high. From the perspective of saving raw materials, 0.25 g of hawthorn powder was used as the optimal dosage. Then, the effect of the dosages of DETA (100, 200, 300 and 400 μL) on the fluorescence intensities was studied. As shown in Fig. S1B, as the dosages of DETA gradually increased, the fluorescence intensities tended to increase first and then decrease. Therefore, a moderate dosage of 300 μL DETA was used as the best dosage. The effect of reaction time from 2 h to 12 h was also investigated. As shown in Fig. S1C, as the reaction time gradually extended, the fluorescence intensities were increased significantly, and it at a reaction time of 12 h was almost the same as that at 10 h. Ultimately, 10 h was chosen as the best response time. Moreover, the effect of reaction temperatures from 120 ℃ to 200 ℃ was preliminarily studied. As shown in Fig. S1D, as the reaction temperature increased, the fluorescence intensities raised gradually. However, when the temperature was 200 ℃, it tended to decrease. We believed that low temperatures may adversely affect the carbonization and surface functionalization of N-CDs. While excessive temperature could lead to the decomposition of N-CDs. Therefore, 180 ℃ was selected as the optimal reaction temperature. Based on the above results, it was concluded that the best conditions for preparing N-CDs were 0.25 g hawthorn powders, 300 μL DETA and 20 mL ultrapure water at 180 ℃ for 10 h.

The N-CDs were characterized by fluorescence spectrophotometer, UV spectrophotometer, TEM, FTIR and XPS. Fig. 2 A showed the UV–visible absorption spectrum, the maximum excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. The UV–visible spectra illustrated the N-CDs had absorption peaks at 320–500 nm, and obvious absorption peak at 340 nm. The well dispersed N-CDs solution was pale yellow under natural light and bright blue colour under UV lamp. The N-CDs exhibited excitation independent emission phenomenon under varied excitation wavelengths between 350 and 400 nm (Fig. 2B). And the N-CDs exhibited an obvious peak around 370 nm. The emission wavelength with the maximum fluorescence intensity was observed at 447 nm upon excitation at 370 nm (Fig. 2A). Furthermore, the N-CDs showed a quantum yield of 22.96%.

The particle morphology and size distribution of the N-CDs were directly described by TEM imaging. In Fig. 2C, the N-CDs were quasi-spherical in shape, uniform in size and well-dispersed. And each nanoparticle had high crystallinity with a lattice parameter of 0.20 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction surface of graphitic (sp2) carbon [33]. In Fig. 2D, based on a count of nearly 1000, the average diameter of the N-CDs was calculated to be 3.17 nm at the range from 1.07 nm to 8.46 nm.

The surface functional groups of the N-CDs were evaluated by the FTIR. In Fig. 3 , the absorption bands around 3400 cm−1 were assigned to the stretching vibrations of the O-H and N-H [21]. The absorption peaks around 2910 cm−1 and 2870 cm−1 corresponded to the C-H stretching vibrations from methyl and methylene groups [34]. The strong absorption bands around 1600 cm−1 could be attributed to the absorption of C=C and –COOH vibrations [35]. The characteristic absorption band around 1450 cm−1 was assigned to C-N and stretched [36]. The peaks around 1290 cm−1 and 1350 cm−1 were the additional peaks of C-N stretching vibration and illustrated the successful doping of nitrogen atoms in the N-CDs [37]. The two absorption bands around 1040 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1 corresponded to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching modes of C-O and indicated C-O oxygen containing functional groups including carboxyl groups [36]. The broad band around 600 cm−1 could be ascribed to N-H. In general, the surface of the N-CDs had unsaturated carbonaceous structure, oxygenated and nitrogen groups, which could enhance the hydrophilicity of the N-CDs.

In order to prove the element types of the N-CDs, XPS was used to analyze the surface composition (Fig. 4 ). In Fig. 4A, the full range XPS analysis of the N-CDs performed C, O, N elements, which were indicated by three peaks at 278.5, 391.5 and 524.5 eV, respectively [38,39]. And the elemental compositions were 67.36%, 18.31% and 14.33%, respectively. In the high resolution XPS spectrum of C1s (Fig. 4B), the presence of C-C, C-N, C-O and C=O was indicated by the four peaks at 284.79, 285.47, 286.28 and 287.85 eV [39]. In the high resolution XPS spectrum of O1s (Fig. 4C), two peaks at 531.39 eV and 533.08 eV were attributed to O-C and O=C, respectively [19]. In the high resolution XPS spectrum of N1s (Fig. 4D), the presence of three peaks at 398.84, 400.11, 401.24 eV was due to C=N-C, N-(C)3, N-H [40]. The results showed the existence of graphitized carbon nuclei, the success of the nitrogen doping and the enrichment of hydrophilic groups (oxygen and nitrogen functional groups) on the surface of the N-CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Building up the assay for CTC
ce:section.ce:para: The CTC was determined by this assay method based on fluorescence quenching. To obtain a wide linear range and high sensitivity, it was necessary to maintain low F/F0. Many possible influencing factors including BR buffer dosage, PH value of BR buffer, N-CDs concentration, time and temperature were explored. Under varying pH values of BR buffer from 5 to 10, the F/F0 was the lowest at pH 8 (Fig. 5 A). Under varying BR buffer dosages from 0.5 to 2 mL, the F/F0 was the lowest at 1 mL (Fig. 5B). Therefore, N-CDs were suitable for more neutral environments. Under the N-CDs concentrations ranged from 1.5 to 15 μg mL−1 (Dilute the liquid 20 to 200 times), the F/F0 was the lowest at 3 μg mL−1 (Fig. 5C). This was due to the high concentration of N-CDs can be not better dispersion. When the reaction temperature was between 15 and 55 ℃, F/F0 gradually increased, so 15 ℃ was the best reaction temperature (Fig. 5D). The temperature dependent fluorescence was attributed to thermal activation of non-radiative trappings in surface and defective sites [41]. Since there was not much difference between the F/F0 values at room temperature and 15 ℃, room temperature was adopted in this experiment for the sake of simplicity. However, F/F0 was almost no change within 40 min (Fig. 5E). In conclusion, the optimized conditions for detections were 3 μg mL−1N-CDs, 1 mL pH 8 BR buffer and room temperature.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Linear range and detection limit of CTC
ce:section.ce:para: According to the above experimental results, a standard curve was established for the quantitative detection method of CTC under the optimal experimental conditions. As shown in Fig. 6 , The fluorescence intensity of N-CDs decreased with the increase of CTC concentration. The fluorescence quenching degree of N-CDs had a good linear relationship with the concentration of CTC in a certain range. The linear range was 0.4–20 μg mL−1. The linear equation was as follows:

Where Y is F/F0, and X is CTC concentration. The correlation coefficient was 0.9992. And the detection limit was 0.073 ± 0.005 μg mL−1 (S/N = 3). Therefore, the method had higher sensitivity and a wider linear range. Furthermore, as shown in Table 1 , the detection range and limit of this method for detecting CTC were comparable or better with other reported assays [42-46]. Due to linear calibration range and low detection limit, it can be developed as suitable assay for CTC residue in pork.

The effects of several antibiotics on the response system were investigated. As shown in Fig. 7 A, chloramphenicol, sulfanilamide, florfenicol had no effect on the reaction system. However, tetracycline group antibiotics (tetracycline, doxycycline and oxytetracycline) showed an effect on the responses, especially tetracycline, which demonstrated that the N-CDs were selective for this kind of antibiotics. Moreover, the influence of common inorganic ions on the reaction system was investigated. As shown in Fig. 7B, none of the above-mentioned substances had a great influence on the system and the interference was small. Only Fe3+ had a significant effect on the fluorescence intensity. F/F0 of the control group without interference was 0.47. When Fe3+ was added into the reaction system, F/F0 was 0.17, which indicated it had a certain interference effect on the reaction system. The fluorescence quenching also occurred when Fe3+ was added into N-CDs system alone. However, the content of Fe3+ was very low in pork samples, mainly in the form of protein chelates. Furthermore, the sample preparation method can be improved later, such as removing metal ions by sulfhydryl cotton. Therefore, it had almost no influence on the establishment of detection methods.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pork sample testing and recovery rate
ce:section.ce:para: For investigating the application of this method, a recovery assay was performed, using pork samples (Table 2 ). The recoveries of three pork samples at different CTC concentration solutions were 93.62%−103.18%, indicating the approach may be promising for CTC detection in pork samples.

Fig. 6A showed that the fluorescence of N-CDs can be effectively quenched by CTC. Usually, fluorescence quenching might be attributed to inner filter effect (IFE), energy transfer and electron transfer [43]. In order to explore the mechanism of fluorescence quenching by this method, a series of studies were carried out. As shown in Fig. 8 A, there was a good overlap between the UV absorption spectrum of CTC and the excitation spectrum of N-CDs. The result indicated that the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs was induced by IFE. Furthermore, no spectral overlap between the fluorescence emission band of N-CDs and the absorption band of CTC was found in Fig. 8A, demonstrating that no energy transfer occurred from N-CDs to CTC [21]. In addition, the UV absorption spectra of CTC, N-CDs, and CTC + N-CDs were compared for further verification. As shown in Fig. 8B, there was no significant difference between the UV absorption spectra of CTC and CTC + N-CDs. This showed that there was no complex formation after adding CTC. So it was no electron transfer between CTC and N-CDs [35]. Therefore, in the presence of CTC, the main mechanism of N-CDs fluorescence quenching was presumed to be IFE. IFE is different from other mechanisms in that it requires neither energy transfer nor electron transfer, which is very simple, flexible [47] and received a lot of attention in recent years. This mechanism of action also provides a fast, sensitive, simple, and highly selective platform for detecting CTC. It provides a new idea for rapid and sensitive detection of antibiotics in food.

In this study, N-CDs with blue fluorescence were prepared by hydrothermally decomposition of hawthorn powder and adding DETA as a nitrogen source. A new fluorescence method for determination of CTC by N-CDs was established based on the phenomenon of fluorescence quenching, which was attributed to inner filter effect. This method was confirmed with a good linear range of 0.4–20 μg mL−1 and low detection limit of 0.073 ± 0.005 μg mL−1. Additionally, chlortetracycline in pork samples had been successfully detected with good recoveries of 93.62%−103.18%. Moreover, the materials and methods of N-CDs synthesis were green, simple and less materials, and the detection process of CTC was also simple to operate, indicating the green, simple and convenient characteristics of the sensor. In a word, it was green, simple, good sensitivity and selectivity, which could be used for rapid detection of CTC content in pork. This study had converted the light absorption signal of CTC into a high-sensitivity fluorescent signal, which provided a green idea for rapid detection of animal-derived food and widen the investigation and application of fluorescent N-CDs for the detection of harmful factors in food.

Huifang Zhang: Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Data curation, Writing - original draft, Visualization. Qian Zhou: Conceptualization, Project administration, Writing - review & editing. Xue Han: Writing - review & editing. Ming Li: Conceptualization. Jing Yuan: Software. Ran Wei: Investigation. Xiaofu Zhang: Data curation. Mengying Wu: Validation. Wen Zhao: Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors have no conflict of interests.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by Hebei Modern Agricultural Industrial Technology System Pig Innovation Team (HBCT 2018110205); and the Food Processing Discipline Group of Hebei Agricultural University (No. 2020-07).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.119736.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Rapid detection and prediction of chlortetracycline and oxytetracycline in animal feed using surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.M. Lee, D. Yarbrough, M.M. Kozman, T.J. Herrman, J. Park, R. Wang, D. Kurouski, Rapid detection and prediction of chlortetracycline and oxytetracycline in animal feed using surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), Food Control 114 (2020) 107243.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Qualitative and quantitative drug residue analyses: Chlortetracycline in white-tailed deer (Odocoileus virginianus) and supermarket meat by liquid chromatography tandem-mass spectrometry

Smith, Qualitative and quantitative drug residue analyses: Chlortetracycline in white-tailed deer (Odocoileus virginianus) and supermarket meat by liquid chromatography tandem-mass spectrometry, J. Chromatogr. B. 1092 (2018) 237-243.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Biochemical studies on streptomyces aureofaciens.iv.studies on the biosynthesis of chlorteracycline

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Jarai, G. Jozsa, J. Kollar, Biochemical studies on streptomyces aureofaciens.iv.studies on the biosynthesis of chlorteracycline, Acta. Microbiol. Acad. Sci.H. 11 (1964) 203-10.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The fate of chlortetracycline residues in a simulated chicken-fish integrated farming systems
